1: The ark remained in Philistine territory for seven months — a specific temporal detail that anchors the narrative in historical memory. Seven months is long enough for the full scope of the plague to develop and the political will to return the ark to solidify. The Philistine lords consulted their own religious specialists: priests and diviners (kōhănîm wəqōsəmîm). The ANE pattern of consulting technical religious experts for plague-management decisions is well-documented in Hittite and Mesopotamian sources.
2: The Philistine priests and diviners are asked: how shall we send back the ark? They insist it must not be sent back empty — it must be accompanied by a guilt offering (ʾāšām). The ʾāšām is the specific reparations-payment for desecration of the sacred in Israel's cultic law (Lev 5-6). The Philistine religious experts are recommending a protocol that aligns closely with Israelite sacral law — whether through direct knowledge of Israelite practice or through the common ANE understanding that sacred objects require proper propitiation.
3: The advice: 'If you send back the ark of the God of Israel, do not send it empty, but by all means return to him a guilt offering. Then you will be healed, and it will be known to you why his hand does not turn from you.' The logic is diagnostic: send the guilt offering not merely as appeasement but as a test — if healing follows, the connection between the ark and the plague is confirmed. This is proto-empirical reasoning applied to a theological question.
4: Five golden tumors (ʿŏpālê zāhāḇ) and five golden mice (ʿakḇārê zāhāḇ) — one per Philistine lord and city. The five cities of the Philistine pentapolis were Ashdod, Gath, Ekron, Ashkelon, and Gaza. The golden replicas of the plague symptoms — tumors and mice — follow ANE sympathetic magic practice: a golden image of the affliction offered to the deity is a way of returning the affliction to its divine source. The mice add the disease-vector detail that supports the bubonic plague identification.
5: 'Make images of your tumors and images of your mice that are ravaging the land, and give glory to the God of Israel. Perhaps he will lighten his hand from you and from your gods and from your land.' 'Give glory' (nəḵaḇḇēḏ) — the verb from kāḇôḏ — is the proper response to YHWH's weight pressing on them. The Philistines are advised to do in action (giving glory through the guilt-offering) what Eli's sons refused to do in worship (1 Samuel 2:29-30: 'those who honor me I will honor').
6: The priests rhetorically invoke Pharaoh: 'Why should you harden your hearts as the Egyptians and Pharaoh hardened their hearts? After he had dealt severely with them, did they not send the people away, and they departed?' The comparison to Pharaoh is striking — the Philistines' own religious specialists counsel against the resistance to YHWH that destroyed Egypt. The Exodus tradition functions here as a warning to the Philistines from within their own religious counsel.
7: The test prescribed: a new cart (ʿăgālāh ḥăḏāšāh) and two nursing cows (pārôṯ ʿālôṯ) that have never been yoked, with their calves shut up at home. The use of animals that have never been yoked mirrors the red heifer requirement (Numbers 19:2) and Deuteronomy 21:3's heifer 'that has never been worked.' In ANE sacred-object transport, ritual purity of the conveyance was required; unused animals were the standard criterion. The calves kept home remove the natural homing instinct as an explanation if the cows head toward Beth-shemesh.
8: The ark is placed on the cart along with the golden guilt offerings in a box beside it. The box (ʾargaz) is a hapax — this specific container term appears only here. Sending the guilt offerings in a separate container alongside the ark respects the boundary between the sacred vessel and the propitiation objects. The entire arrangement is the Philistine priests' best attempt at proper sacred transport.
9: The Philistines prescribe an observable test: 'Watch. If it goes up on the road to its own territory, toward Beth-shemesh, then it is he who has done us this great harm; but if not, then we shall know that it is not his hand that struck us — it happened to us by coincidence.' The explicit formulation of an observable criterion for distinguishing divine causation from coincidence is a remarkable epistemological moment. The Philistine priests are constructing a controlled test of their hypothesis.
10: The Philistines do as instructed: two nursing cows on the new cart, calves shut up at home. The detail of the calves being secured is the control element — the maternal bond should pull the cows homeward, not toward a distant Israelite town. Any deviation from that natural pull would be interpretable as supernatural direction.
11: They put the ark of YHWH on the cart and the box with the golden mice and golden tumors. The entire guilt-offering apparatus is loaded — nothing is left behind. The Philistine priests have prepared the reparations as completely as their knowledge allows. The departure of the cart from Philistine territory is the climax of the ark narrative's first half.
12: The cows go straight (wayyišṣarnāh — the verb from yāšār, straight/direct) on the road to Beth-shemesh, mooing as they go, not turning to the right or to the left. The mothers' lowing (wayyiggāʿagnāh) as they walk away from their calves makes the supernatural direction audible: every instinct says turn back, but they go straight ahead. The Philistine lords follow behind as far as the border of Beth-shemesh — witnessing the test's outcome.
13: The people of Beth-shemesh are harvesting wheat in the valley when they see the ark coming — and rejoice. Beth-shemesh ('house of the sun god') was an Israelite city in the Sorek Valley, at the boundary between Israelite and Philistine territory — precisely the cultural-geographic boundary the ark crossed. The wheat harvest provides the flat threshing floor (v14) and the timing: late spring, when the agricultural calendar created public gatherings of the whole community.
14: The cart comes into the field of Joshua of Beth-shemesh and stops near a large stone. The Levites take down the ark of YHWH and the box of golden objects and place them on the large stone. The wood of the cart becomes firewood and the cows become a burnt offering. The large stone functions as an impromptu altar — the cart becomes its own sacrificial pyre. The spontaneous burnt offering of the transport animals is a response of gratitude without prior planning.
15: Levites come down from the cart — the text assumes Levites are present at Beth-shemesh, consistent with the Levitical cities distribution (Joshua 21:16 includes Beth-shemesh as a Levitical city). The Levites are the proper handlers of the sacred objects; the narrative's mention of them preserves the requirement that sacred transport required qualified personnel. The men of Beth-shemesh offer burnt offerings and make sacrifices on that day.
16: The five Philistine lords see the events and return to Ekron that same day. Their witness-departure confirms that the test was observed by Philistine authorities. They have seen the empirical evidence — the cows went straight to Israelite territory, the ark was received by Israelites — and their theological hypothesis is confirmed. They return without further incident; the diplomatic crisis is resolved.
17: The golden tumors: one for Ashdod, one for Gaza, one for Ashkelon, one for Gath, one for Ekron — the full pentapolis accounted for. The specific enumeration of all five cities is the narrator's way of confirming that the guilt-offering covered the full geographic scope of the plague. Gaza and Ashkelon do not appear in the plague narrative itself but are included in the guilt-offering — either because the plague did spread there (narrative compression) or as a preventive inclusion.
18: The golden mice: according to the number of all the cities of the Philistines belonging to the five lords, both fortified cities and unwalled villages. The extension beyond the five city-lords to include dependent villages suggests that the plague's impact was regional, not limited to the five major urban centers. The large stone on which the ark rested became a memorial in the field of Joshua of Beth-shemesh — a specific topographical marker preserved in local memory.
19: YHWH struck down some of the men of Beth-shemesh because they looked into the ark of YHWH. The number in the MT is 'seventy men' (some manuscripts add 50,000, creating the impossible '50,070' of many translations — almost certainly a scribal error combining two variant readings). Seventy is the plausible number for a local incident. The transgression — looking into (rāʾāh bə) the ark — violates the strict prohibition on unauthorized viewing of the ark's contents (Numbers 4:20). Curiosity about the sacred does not constitute authorization.
20: The people of Beth-shemesh ask: 'Who is able to stand before YHWH, this holy God (ʾĕlōhîm qādôš)?' and send messengers to the inhabitants of Kiriath-jearim: 'The Philistines have returned the ark of YHWH. Come down and take it up to you.' The question — 'who is able to stand?' — is not rhetorical despair but a practical liturgical query: who has the proper cultic standing to host YHWH's ark? Beth-shemesh recognizes it cannot. The question will not be permanently answered until David's elaborate ark-procession in 2 Sam 6.
21: The messengers go to Kiriath-jearim — 'Kiriath of the forests,' modern Abu Ghosh or Tell el-Azhar, in the hill country of Judah near the Benjamin border. Kiriath-jearim is at the appropriate geographical and cultic distance from Beth-shemesh: far enough from Philistine territory, in the highlands rather than the valley, and accessible from the central hill country. It will house the ark for twenty years (1 Samuel 7:2) until David's transfer to Jerusalem (2 Samuel 6:2).
